preFix.title: Botanical impression slab tile
preFix.subtitle: A flat clay tile with pressed plant impressions
preFix.excerpt: A flat air-dry clay tile with impressions from real plant material pressed into the surface. Fern fronds, leaf veins, and flower petals all transfer their detail into soft clay. The finished tile works as a wall hanging, a trivet for dry items, or a decorative plaque. No kiln required.
preFix.sourceNotes: Botanical impression technique derived from first principles. Nature-printing into clay is documented in nineteenth-century craft manuals.

content.text: Botanical impressions in clay are one of the oldest forms of surface decoration. Pressing a real leaf into a soft
slab

transfers every vein and edge detail into the clay surface. Air-dry clay records the impression permanently as it hardens. No kiln is required. The finished tile works as a wall plaque, a shelf display, or a dry trivet. Do not use as a hot mat or a surface for wet items.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay for better impression detail

content.text: Choosing plant material

Flat leaves with distinct veining give the clearest impressions: ferns, oak leaves, ivy, mint, hornbeam, and plane leaves all work well. Thick, fleshy leaves compress too much and blur. Fresh leaves press more deeply than dried because they are pliable. Test on a small scrap of clay before committing to the full tile.
Rolling the slab

Roll clay between the slab guides to 8 mm thickness. Cut a tile of the desired shape: a square 150 mm by 150 mm, a rectangle 100 mm by 200 mm, or an irregular shape following the outline of one large leaf. A 8 mm thickness is the minimum for a tile that will be handled and hung without cracking.
Pressing the impressions

Lay the plant material on the clay surface. Place the leaf vein-side down for the deepest impression, as the veins are the raised structures that transfer best. Cover the leaf with a sheet of paper to protect the clay surface and roll over it once with the rolling pin, pressing firmly. Peel back the paper and then the leaf slowly from one edge. The impression is now in the clay.

Build up a composition with multiple leaves and stems overlapping. The layering of impressions creates depth. For a centred composition, start with the largest leaf in the middle and add smaller leaves and stems radiating outward.
Adding a hanging hole

Pierce one or two holes near the top edge of the tile with the needle tool while the clay is still soft. Make each hole about 5 mm in diameter. Thread a length of cord or ribbon through when the tile is finished.
Drying
Leave the
greenware

tile flat on the board for 24-48 hours. If curling begins during the first few hours, lay a flat board on top to keep the tile flat. Turn the tile over once the top surface is firm, so the underside dries evenly. The tile is ready when it feels
bone-dry
and no longer cool to the touch.
Finishing

Sand the edges and back of the tile lightly with fine sandpaper. Apply acrylic paint if a coloured finish is desired; a wash of earthy ochre or green tinted paint over the impressed surface and wiped back from the high points creates a two-tone effect that emphasises the botanical detail. Seal with two coats of clear acrylic varnish.
Variations
Cut the tile in the outline of a single large leaf for a leaf-shaped plaque.
Press feathers, grasses, or bark rubbings into the surface for different textures and seasonal themes.
Make a set of four tiles using leaves from the same tree in different seasons for a botanical series.

rows.fix: Press vein-side down and roll firmly. Test on a clay scrap before the main tile.
rows.cause: Leaf placed upside down (smooth side down instead of vein side), or too little pressure applied

rows.fix: Roll to an even 8 mm. Cover loosely with plastic for the first 12 hours for a slower dry.
rows.cause: Uneven slab thickness, or the tile dried too fast on one surface
rows.problem: Tile cracks across the middle during drying

rows.fix: Allow the slab to stiffen for 10-15 minutes after rolling before pressing the botanicals.
rows.cause: Clay was too wet and gripped the leaf
rows.problem: Plant material tears when peeling away

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
